chapters.title: The perfect startup myth
chapters.text:
Imagine a scene of young, vibrant college students huddled together in a dorm room, their faces illuminated by the glow of their laptops. They're crafting the next big thing — a revolutionary product that's bound to change the world.

Eric Ries found himself in a similar scene a decade ago, dreaming big and believing the world would soon be at his feet from all his hard work. But before long, he stood with his co-founder in the rain, arguing in the street, realizing their promising startup was about to crumble. Although they had a great idea and a dedicated team, they lacked a crucial element — an understanding of how to turn their products into thriving businesses.

Undoubtedly, dealing with failure is challenging. But the truth is that most startups don't make it despite the media's stories glorifying successful entrepreneurs. Most new products never find their market. And yet, these tales still captivate people who believe they can succeed with “the right stuff.”

In light of this, Ries's career, with its successes and failures, reveals that the key to startup success is not just innate talent or perfect timing. Rather, it comes from systematic processes and ongoing learning.

Wild ideas need effective management to lead to success.

Ries started a new startup, IMVU, a few years after the initial setback. This time, the company focused on quick improvements rather than perfection, valued customer feedback, and embraced experimentation. This revolutionary approach led to what Ries termed the “Lean Startup” methodology — a proven way to turn big ideas into reality.

So, let's start a journey to success by shedding outdated myths and embracing a future of systematic entrepreneurship!

chapters.title: A progressive approach to managing startups
chapters.text:
Launching a business is like building a house: you need a plan and control to make creative ideas thrive. But the thing is that old management rules rarely work for startups. They can slow down the progress when it must be quick and flexible. The outdated approach aimed at fitting unique entrepreneurial challenges into the box of general management has led many startups down a path of disorder and failure.

There are more people today trying to start businesses than ever before. Take the US as an example. Even though there's been a dip in manufacturing jobs, the output from those factories is still rising. How? New technology and smart management are the answer. It shows that we can do more with less.

But without a solid game plan for these new businesses, so much potential goes down the drain. Some products receive a lot of publicity only to disappear in a flash. And it's about more than money lost. Think about the dreams, time, and effort of all those involved. Such a waste, right?

The key to successful entrepreneurship is having a clear strategy for implementing the idea.

The Lean Startup is a way to improve this situation. It draws inspiration from Toyota's manufacturing practices, shaking things up in how startups work. Just like the original approach made factories smarter by cutting waste and upping quality, it fits those ideas into entrepreneurship.

Traditional industries often measure success by how much they produce. With The Lean Startup, it's all about “validated learning.” It means checking in with customers, iterating on ideas, and ensuring businesses create products that people really want. Business owners' lives are uncertain, so having a comprehensive game plan that addresses everything is crucial.

It is essential to understand that entrepreneurship isn't just for startup founders. Even in big companies, many people have that creative drive. They have vision, take risks, and know how to organize. Take Mark, a forward-thinking manager charged with developing a suite of products that would allow the company to take advantage of the Internet. Despite knowing how to build innovative teams and implement technology, he lacked the overarching principle to make creative ideas work.

That's where “The Lean Startup” comes in. It offers a clear path for entrepreneurs, guiding them from hypothesis to action.

chapters.title: Growing through learning and experimenting
chapters.text:
The startup world often requires delving into areas of uncertainty and ambiguity. However, one of the critical principles that helps a startup pivot on its path toward success is learning. As many entrepreneurs venture into new territories, they may make certain assumptions about their product or target audience. Such beliefs might not always resonate with what the customers truly desire.

Take, for instance, the experience of IMVU. Their strategy seemed innovative: integrating features of 3D video games into an instant messaging environment. Yet, despite their efforts and constant improvements, the uptake remained minimal.

After digging deeper, IMVU found a mismatch between what they thought customers wanted and what they delivered. The company had imagined people would eagerly invite friends to join. In reality, users were hesitant to bring buddies onto a platform they weren't sure about. Only when they deemed IMVU “cool” did they consider inviting others.

Another crucial moment for IMVU was discovering users wanted to meet new friends online rather than chat with current ones. In other words, the company's perception of the product differs from the consumer's.

Startups provide ideas for how to build a successful business.

The best way to understand what customers want is to do experiments. Take Zappos' story, for instance. Its founder, Nick Swinmurn, believed people were ready to buy shoes online; he decided to test his hypothesis. He approached local shoe stores with a proposal: let him photograph their inventory, and if anyone bought a pair online, he'd come back and purchase the shoes at full price. This simple experiment set the stage for the future of online shoe shopping. Zappos started with a straightforward question: “Would people buy shoes online?” It allowed the team to engage with real customers and partners, gathering invaluable insights. It's one thing to have numbers and data, but understanding the human aspect — the preferences, needs, and feedback of customers — is equally vital.

This success story highlights the power of checking your assumptions. When startups first embrace this mindset of testing, they move from theory to practice. It paves the way for gathering an early user base, shaping the offering depending on feedback, and crafting a solution that truly addresses a need. So, when a product hits the market, it's already supported by real-world insights.

Did you know? According to Demand Sage for 2023, 15.4% of the US population works for startups.

chapters.title: Start simple and seize your success
chapters.text:
Every business begins with fundamental assumptions that help shape a company's plans. These are called “leaps of faith” — bold assertions that the entire venture's success hinges on. These beliefs can pave the way for tremendous opportunities or total failure.

Often, businesses compare their ideas to other companies or technologies. They say, “Our product is like that successful one from before, so ours will be prosperous too.” While this method is helpful, it can also be misleading. Therefore, it is more reliable to validate ideas through empirical testing.

Think about Steve Jobs. Before the iPod, Sony's Walkman showed that people liked music on the go. But Napster also showed that clients dislike paying for music downloads. Steve Jobs had to make the leap of faith: Would people pay for music on the iPod? They did, and the iPod was a hit.

Creating a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) can help startups test their assumptions. It is a prototype of a future invention or technology that does not aim for perfection but to check customers' reactions.

Make MVPs as simple as possible — they should be the fastest way to get feedback.

Let's take Dropbox's story as an example. Drew Houston, the mind behind the company, created a file-sharing tool that synchronized files easily across devices. The idea was simple: offer users a seamless way to manage their docs. Yet, with any new concept, the big question was, “How do we show people they need this?”

Instead of spending time and resources on building a complete app, Drew took a different path. He made a short, three-minute video to demonstrate how Dropbox would work. The response was massive. The video boosted Dropbox's waiting list from 5,000 to a whopping 75,000! But this was about more than just getting likes or views. It served as a Minimum Viable Product. Dropbox's experience shows the value of starting simple. Drew didn't wait for a perfect product. He tested his idea, confirming its potential and setting the stage for the company's success.

MVPs are just the beginning. They're the first step in tweaks, changes, and growth. Sometimes, some parts of the product don't match what users want. That's when you can pivot or change direction, keeping user needs in focus. So, whether in tech or any field, remember that sometimes the simplest test can show the biggest possibilities.

When in doubt, simplify. ~ Eric Ries

chapters.title: The power of the right choices
chapters.text:
In the past, big companies, like General Motors, used accounting to keep track of different parts of their business. It was a way to set goals and check their progress. However, this old-school approach is unsuitable for startups because they are impossible to predict. That's where Innovation Accounting comes in.

It’s a new way of looking at financial management that operates in three stages:

1. Establishing the Baseline: Use a simple version of your product (MVP) to see where you stand. The MVP can test different things or only the main. This step will help provide a clear picture of the current situation.
2. Tuning the Engine: With your basic data, try tweaking things to get closer to your ideal business.
3. Pivoting or persevering: After making changes, decide whether to stick to your plan or try something new. If changing direction works better, you made a successful pivot.

Adaptability can turn challenges into opportunities.

The word “pivot” stands tall in the complex realm of startups. It's a thoughtful shift to explore the foundational beliefs of a product, its business approach, or how it grows.

Let's take the story of David Binetti, the CEO of Votizen, to see this process in action. Binetti started Votizen to boost political involvement. He envisioned a special place where voters could discuss civic matters. The concept was fresh but wasn't growing as he hoped.

Rather than getting disheartened, David Binetti took inspiration from the “Lean Startup” philosophy. He continually refined his product, connected with potential users, and organized focus groups. Even after spending eight months and $20,000, it still failed to reach his growth goals. It's a challenge many entrepreneurs face: should they modify, stay the course, or try something new?

Based on user feedback, Binetti found that while they appreciated the voter verification feature, social networking needed improvement. In response, David reshaped Votizen into a tool letting users communicate with their elected officials via existing social platforms.

After another four months and an additional $30,000, things looked better. Yet, profits didn't necessarily guarantee success. So, David changed direction again, focusing not on individual users but on big organizations interested in politics. He shifted from targeting everyday consumers (B2C) to businesses (B2B). Today, Votizen is doing well, but look at all the twists and turns they went through! Their story highlights a startup's fluid nature and need for flexible thinking.

chapters.title: The keys to sustainable growth
chapters.text:
In today's world, people may believe producing several things at once is best. But doing one task from start to finish can often be quicker. Take the example of James Womack and his daughters, who stuffed envelopes. They discovered that completing one envelope was faster than the intuitive method of preparing each stage in large batches.

Imagine if you found out that the letters were too big for the envelopes after you had stuffed a bunch of them. That's a huge waste of time. But if you did them separately, you'd see the problem immediately.

This idea has also changed big industries. People like Taiichi Ohno and Shigeo Shingo used this method at Toyota, a major automobile manufacturer. After World War II, Toyota lacked the resources to make cars like American factories. So, they decided to make them in small batches. It allowed them to make different types of cars and quickly change their machines if needed. Their emphasis on rapidly adjusting and Shingo's SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) concept was crucial in ensuring efficient small-batch production.

Learn faster in business by taking one small step at a time.

The question of growth is crucial for any startup. Ries recalls how two businesses asked him for advice. The first was a marketplace for collectibles, hoping to compete with giants like eBay. The second offered cutting-edge database software for major corporations. Both seemed promising, having revenue and satisfied customers. However, they shared a challenge: their development had halted. This decision was rooted in sustainable growth that previous clients could fuel.

There are four main ways that past customers spark sustainable progress:

• Word of mouth: When someone enthusiastically shares their latest purchase, it piques interest, leading others to explore and buy.
• Product usage side effects: Think of luxury cars or trendy fashion. When someone spots these, it influences their purchasing decisions.
• Paid advertising: Many companies use ads to lure customers. But for long-term growth, these ads should be financed by the company's earnings.
• Repeat purchases: Products or services people use regularly, like monthly subscriptions or daily essentials.

These methods offer actionable steps for businesses to see tangible growth. Success comes from picking the right source of progress for your business and optimizing it.

Reading is good, action is better. ~ Eric Ries

chapters.title: Fueling progress through adaptability and innovation
chapters.text:
Constant changes in the business world can make startup leaders wonder if they're on the right path. And while entirely avoiding risks is impossible, there is a way to make new twists easier and safer.

Let's consider the story of IMVU. Instead of fitting into traditional molds, they focused on becoming an adaptive organization. It meant they not only reacted to the market but evolved based on learned insights. They recognized the importance of training and invested in a program that made new hires productive from day one. This strategy emerged from a consistent approach to refining their process.

Asking profound questions can reveal underlying problems.

The “Five Whys” technique is a helpful tool for startups. Developed by Taiichi Ohno for Toyota, it means asking “Why?” five times to uncover the core issue. Let's take a machine malfunction as an example. If you ask “Why?” you may discover a blown fuse. Digging deeper could reveal problems ranging from inadequate lubrication to missing parts, highlighting technical and human mistakes.

IMVU's experience serves as a case in point. When a new product led to customer complaints, using the Five Whys traced the issue from a technical glitch to an untrained staff member and even to management's attitude towards training. It revealed that behind many technical issues often lie human errors and provided a way to adapt to this reality.

Growing companies often grapple with the challenge of maintaining their spark of innovation. But how can companies try new ideas without taking huge risks? Enter the “innovation sandbox.” Think of it as a playground for ideas. Teams can test things out, see what works, and make changes.

Here's how this works:

• Teams can test out ideas, but only in certain areas. For example, a website might only experiment on some pages. A store might try out new things in just a few locations.
• One team takes charge of each test from start to finish.
• Tests have a set time to run. Some might be short, others a bit longer.
• Only a certain number of customers will see these tests.
• The results are checked using five to ten clear measures.
• All teams use these same measures to gauge their performance.
• Teams watch how customers react. If something's not working, they'll stop the test.

In the end, it's more than just trying out new concepts. It's about giving teams a safe space to learn and grow. With new challenges popping up, this sandbox is the perfect place to test and refine ideas, paving the way for future success.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Starting a business is a dynamic process akin to constructing a resilient building. You need the right tools, materials, and, sometimes, a new blueprint when things don't go as planned. But sometimes, the usual ways don't work for startups. It can lead to problems. Instead, think about what companies like Toyota did. They found new ways to work. Businesses can make products people want by learning from real-life experiences rather than just making stuff. This way of thinking helps new and old companies move from ideas to real results.

Stepping into business means constantly discovering and sometimes changing directions. Look at IMVU and Zappos. They teach us that testing ideas can save a lot of effort. Starting with an MVP can be a good move as a business grows. It's not about being perfect immediately but about trying things out.

Every business starts with creative concepts. But it is essential to test these hypotheses against real situations. A basic product version helps in getting feedback quickly. Remember, the path to success has its bumps. Sometimes, you must change directions entirely.

One thing remains key throughout this rollercoaster: the ability to adapt and innovate. Encourage a space where questions lead to deeper insights and fresh ideas are always brewing.

Try this



1. Start your business with an MVP. Make it simple and adjust to clients' feedback.
2. If you have a problem, ask “Why?” five times to discover its root cause.
3. Give your team a space to try out new ideas safely.
4. Be ready to adapt as the business world changes.
